title: Prioritization Technique Selection Guide
content: Technique selection: MoSCoW for quick categorization low effort early stages; Kano for UX/product feature expectations; WSJF for Lean/SAFe economic prioritization high objectivity; RICE for product management growth features; Cost of Delay for time-sensitive strategic decisions; Value/Effort for quick relative ranking sprint planning; 100-Point for stakeholder consensus workshops.
tags: prioritization technique selection MoSCoW WSJF RICE Kano comparison

title: MoSCoW Method Definition
content: MoSCoW: Must Have (cannot go live without; legal/regulatory; core business; safety minimums); Should Have (important not critical; painful workaround exists); Could Have (nice to have; easy workaround; include if budget permits); Won't Have this time (explicitly out of scope; documented for future). Budget ratio: 60/20/10 Must/Should/Could.
tags: MoSCoW must-have should-have could-have wont-have method prioritization

title: MoSCoW Must Have Test
content: Must Have test: "If we don't have this can we go live?" → NO = Must Have. Characteristics: system cannot function without it; legal or regulatory requirement; core business process enabler; safety or security minimum. Typically 60% of effort budget. If everything is Must Have apply the 60% rule.
tags: must-have MoSCoW test criteria go-live legal regulatory core

title: MoSCoW Should Have Test
content: Should Have test: "Is there a realistic workaround?" → YES (but painful) = Should Have. Characteristics: important but not critical for launch; workaround exists but causes friction; significant business value; typically represents 20% of effort budget.
tags: should-have MoSCoW test criteria workaround business-value

title: MoSCoW Could Have and Won't Have
content: Could Have: "Would users be disappointed without it?" → Minor disappointment = Could Have. Nice to have; enhances experience; easy workaround; 10% of effort budget. Won't Have: "Can this wait for next release?" → YES. Explicitly out of scope; documented for future; manages expectations transparently.
tags: could-have wont-have MoSCoW out-of-scope future-release scope-management

title: MoSCoW Pitfalls and Solutions
content: MoSCoW pitfalls: Everything is Must (no prioritization → enforce 60/20/10 ratio); No Won't items (scope creep → require explicit Won't list); Stakeholder gaming (inflate to Must → use objective business rule criteria); Static categories (priorities change → re-evaluate each release).
tags: MoSCoW pitfalls scope-creep ratio gaming static solutions

title: WSJF Formula Weighted Shortest Job First
content: WSJF = Cost of Delay / Job Duration. Cost of Delay = User/Business Value + Time Criticality + Risk Reduction/Opportunity Enablement. All components scored 1-20 using modified Fibonacci (1 2 3 5 8 13 20). Job Size scored 1-20 relative effort. Higher WSJF = higher priority. SAFe standard.
tags: WSJF formula SAFe cost-of-delay job-size fibonacci prioritization

title: WSJF Cost of Delay Components
content: WSJF Cost of Delay has 3 components: 1.User/Business Value (1-20): revenue potential cost savings market share protection; 2.Time Criticality (1-20): deadline exists value decays over time competitive pressure; 3.Risk Reduction/Opportunity Enablement (1-20): reduces risk for other features enables future revenue technical debt reduction.
tags: WSJF cost-of-delay business-value time-criticality risk-reduction components

title: WSJF Scoring Example
content: WSJF example: Feature B (BV=8 TC=13 RR=8 CoD=29 Size=3 WSJF=9.7 Rank1) beats Feature A (BV=13 TC=8 RR=5 CoD=26 Size=5 WSJF=5.2 Rank2). Decision order B→A→D→C. Workshop: calibrate scale first; use relative sizing; timebox 2min per feature; average if no consensus.
tags: WSJF example scoring ranking workshop relative-sizing fibonacci

title: Kano Model Five Categories
content: Kano Model categories: Basic/Must-be (expected taken for granted; absence = dissatisfaction; presence = neutral); Performance/One-dimensional (more = more satisfaction linear); Delighters/Attractive (unexpected; absence = neutral; presence = high satisfaction); Indifferent; Reverse (some users dislike feature). Over time: Delighters→Performance→Basic.
tags: Kano model categories basic performance delighter indifferent reverse satisfaction

title: Kano Basic Must-Be Requirements
content: Kano Basic (Must-be): customers expect these; won't thank you for having them but WILL punish you for missing. Examples: login security data saving stable performance. These must be satisfied before investing in delighters. Absence causes dissatisfaction regardless of other features.
tags: Kano basic must-be expectations hygiene factors dissatisfaction threshold

title: Kano Performance One-Dimensional
content: Kano Performance: linear relationship between implementation level and satisfaction. More = better satisfaction; less = less satisfaction. Examples: load time search accuracy price. Investment in these produces proportional return. Benchmark against competitors to set targets.
tags: Kano performance one-dimensional linear satisfaction benchmark competitive

title: Kano Delighters Attractive Features
content: Kano Delighters (Attractive): customers didn't know they wanted it but love it when present. High impact on satisfaction with relatively low effort. Examples: proactive suggestions auto-fill personalization. Key insight: over time delighters become performance features then basic (commoditization). First-mover advantage.
tags: Kano delighter attractive excitement unexpected satisfaction commoditization

title: RICE Formula
content: RICE Score = (Reach × Impact × Confidence) / Effort. Reach = users/transactions per time period (actual count). Impact scale: 3=Massive 2=High 1=Medium 0.5=Low 0.25=Minimal. Confidence: 100%=data-backed 80%=some data 50%=gut feeling. Effort = person-months. Higher score = higher priority.
tags: RICE formula reach impact confidence effort prioritization product-management

title: RICE Calculation Example
content: RICE example: One-click buy (50000 users × 2 impact × 80% × /3 months = 26667); Wish list (30000 × 1 × 80% / 1 = 24000); Dark mode (20000 × 0.5 × 100% / 1 = 10000); AI recommend (50000 × 2 × 50% / 8 = 6250). Insight: AI drops due to low confidence and high effort despite high reach.
tags: RICE example calculation confidence effort penalize wishful-thinking

title: Cost of Delay Linear Profile
content: Cost of Delay Linear (Standard): constant value per unit time; each week of delay costs same amount. Example $10K/week lost revenue. Use when feature has stable ongoing value. Calculate: expected revenue per month × delay months. If CoD > development cost prioritize aggressively.
tags: cost-of-delay linear standard profile calculation revenue WSJF

title: Cost of Delay Urgent Deadline Profile
content: Cost of Delay Urgent (Deadline): full value before deadline; drops to zero after. Miss the window lose everything. Examples: seasonal features regulatory deadlines competitive windows. Must be delivered on time or deprioritized entirely. Drive with binary decision: can we hit the deadline?
tags: cost-of-delay urgent deadline binary profile seasonal regulatory competitive

title: Cost of Delay Fixed Date and Decay Profiles
content: Cost of Delay Fixed Date: no value before launch date then full value (regulatory compliance contract deadlines). Intangible/Decay: value decays over time; first-mover advantage; competitive features. Four profiles: Linear Urgent Fixed-Date Decay. Match profile to feature type for accurate WSJF.
tags: cost-of-delay fixed-date decay profiles first-mover competitive WSJF SAFe

title: Cost of Delay Calculation Factors
content: Cost of Delay factors: Revenue Gain (expected revenue/month × delay months); Revenue Protection (at-risk revenue × probability of loss); Cost Savings (monthly savings × delay months); Cost of Workaround (manual effort cost/month × delay months); Penalty/Fine (regulatory penalty if missed); Competitive Loss (market share loss × CLV).
tags: cost-of-delay calculation revenue savings penalty competitive factors

title: Value Effort Matrix
content: Value/Effort 2x2 Matrix: Q1 Quick Wins (high value low effort → DO FIRST); Q2 Big Bets (high value high effort → plan strategically); Q3 Fill-Ins (low value low effort → do if capacity allows); Q4 Time Sinks (low value high effort → AVOID unless required). Priority order: 1→2→3→4.
tags: value-effort matrix quick-wins big-bets fill-ins time-sinks prioritization

title: Weighted Criteria Matrix 3-6-9 Scoring
content: Weighted Criteria Matrix: define criteria (business value risk compliance UX performance); assign weights (must sum to 100%); score each feature per criterion using 3-6-9 scale (3=low fit 6=medium 9=high fit); multiply score×weight; sum for total weighted score; rank by total. Removes subjectivity.
tags: weighted-criteria matrix 3-6-9 scoring weights criteria ranking objectivity

title: Cost-Benefit Analysis Procedure
content: Cost-Benefit procedure: 1.List all costs (development infrastructure training support); 2.List all benefits (revenue increase cost reduction risk reduction competitive advantage); 3.Quantify both in monetary terms; 4.Calculate NPV (Net Present Value) or ROI; 5.Compare break-even period; 6.Decide if benefits outweigh costs over defined horizon.
tags: cost-benefit analysis NPV ROI break-even procedure quantify decision

title: Value Cost Risk Priority Formula
content: Priority Score = (Business Value × Value Weight) + (Cost Savings × Cost Weight) - (Risk × Risk Weight). Weights defined by organization. Risk reduces priority; value and savings increase it. Normalize scores 1-100 for comparison. Document weighting rationale for stakeholder alignment.
tags: value-cost-risk formula priority-score weights normalization business-value

title: Timeboxing Approach for Prioritization
content: Timeboxing: fix time budget (sprint release); prioritize features to fit within box; cut scope not quality. Steps: estimate all features; sort by priority score; fill timebox from top until capacity reached; everything below line = Won't Have this iteration. Re-evaluate next timebox.
tags: timeboxing sprint release capacity scope-management prioritization agile

title: Change Request Prioritization
content: CR priority levels: Critical/Blocking (system unusable; fix immediately); High (major functionality impaired; fix this sprint); Medium (important improvement; plan next sprint); Low (minor enhancement; backlog). CR prioritization considers: business impact; number of users affected; workaround availability; regulatory requirement.
tags: change-request CR prioritization critical high medium low impact

title: Dependencies Impact on Prioritization
content: Dependency types: Technical (B requires A to be built first); Business (C has no value without D live); Regulatory (E must launch before F); Resource (G and H need same team). Dependencies can force higher-priority items lower or lower-priority items higher. Map dependency graph before finalizing order.
tags: dependencies prioritization technical business regulatory sequencing ordering

title: Prioritization Workshop Agenda
content: Workshop agenda (2 hours): 0-15min introduction ground rules objectives; 15-30min review features list shared understanding; 30-60min initial categorization MoSCoW or Value/Effort; 60-90min detailed scoring WSJF or RICE; 90-105min discussion adjustments consensus; 105-120min document decisions next steps.
tags: prioritization-workshop agenda template 2-hours MoSCoW WSJF RICE facilitation

title: Prioritization Quality Checklist
content: Checklist: Preparation (unique IDs; stakeholders understand technique; value sources defined; estimates available); Execution (consistent criteria; rationale documented; dependencies considered; conflicts resolved); Validation (distribution reasonable not all Must; sponsor approved; team aligned; re-prioritization triggers defined); Documentation (rationale in requirements; deferred items listed; communicated).
tags: prioritization checklist preparation execution validation documentation quality

title: 100-Point Voting Method
content: 100-Point method: each stakeholder gets 100 points; distributes among features; cannot give more than 30 points to one item (prevents monopoly). Tally points across stakeholders; rank by total. Fast for workshops; captures collective priority. Limitation: doesn't capture economic value or dependencies. Good for consensus-building.
tags: 100-point voting method workshop consensus stakeholder facilitation

title: Buy a Feature Game
content: Buy a Feature: features have fictional prices based on complexity; stakeholders given budget to purchase; discuss trade-offs to buy most desired features within budget. Reveals true priorities through economic choices. Good for product discovery engagement. Limitation: prices may not reflect real complexity.
tags: buy-a-feature game product-discovery engagement workshop prioritization

title: WSJF Workshop Script Steps
content: WSJF workshop: 1.Calibrate scale (identify biggest item=20 smallest=1 for each factor); 2.Relative sizing not absolute; 3.Use modified Fibonacci 1-2-3-5-8-13-20; 4.Timebox 2min per feature; 5.If no consensus average and move on; 6.Break ties by dependencies when WSJF within 10%.
tags: WSJF workshop script calibrate Fibonacci timebox consensus relative-sizing

title: Prioritization Economics Principle
content: Key insight: prioritization is an economic decision. Wrong order has real cost. Example: Feature A=$100K value; Feature B=$50K value; building B before A causes A to be delayed 1 extra month = $25K lost vs $12.5K if built in right order. Difference = $12.5K savings from correct sequencing alone.
tags: prioritization economics cost-of-wrong-order sequencing value delay opportunity-cost

title: Kano Classification Survey Method
content: Kano survey asks two questions per feature: Functional (if this feature IS present how do you feel? Like/Expect/Neutral/Tolerate/Dislike) and Dysfunctional (if this feature IS NOT present how do you feel? Like/Expect/Neutral/Tolerate/Dislike). Cross-reference responses in Kano matrix to classify as A/O/M/I/R/Q.
tags: Kano survey functional dysfunctional classification matrix A O M I R Q
